title: Postnatal Check
description: Percentage of women aged 15 to 49 years who had a live birth in the 2 years preceding the survey who received a postnatal check during the first 2 days after birth, by region. Source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS).

definitions.postnatal_check_pct: Includes women who received a check from a doctor, nurse, midwife, traditional birth attendant/hilot, or barangay health worker.

note: Details may not add up to total due to rounding.

insights.title: Insights on Postnatal Check
insights.subtitle: AI-generated insights based on National Demographic and Health Survey data on postnatal check
insights.overview: Whether a woman receives a postnatal check soon after delivery still depends heavily on where she lives. In 2025, Western Visayas reached 91.4 percent of women receiving a check within the first 2 days after birth, while BARMM was at 54.1 percent. Cagayan Valley and MIMAROPA were also well below the national total of 76.6 percent, showing how uneven the first days after birth remain across the country.

sections.heading: A small group of regions is clearly ahead
sections.body: Western Visayas led at 91.4 percent in 2025, followed by Davao Region at 89.0 percent, Northern Mindanao at 86.5 percent, and Caraga at 86.2 percent. At the other end, BARMM was at 54.1 percent, Cagayan Valley at 61.2 percent, and MIMAROPA at 62.0 percent. That is not a narrow spread. It means mothers in some regions are much more likely than others to be seen soon after birth.

sections.heading: The national average hides large regional shifts
sections.body: The national figure changed only slightly, from 75.4 percent in 2022 to 76.6 percent in 2025. Underneath that stable average, several regions moved sharply. Ilocos Region rose from 62.6 percent to 81.1 percent, while BARMM rose from 44.5 percent to 54.1 percent and NCR from 68.6 percent to 76.2 percent. But Cagayan Valley fell from 79.9 percent to 61.2 percent, MIMAROPA from 75.8 percent to 62.0 percent, and Central Visayas from 89.3 percent to 77.5 percent. The national trend is therefore much calmer than the regional story.

sections.heading: Education marks a clearer divide than age does
sections.body: In 2025, women with Grades 1-6 education were at 68.7 percent, compared with 75.3 percent among those with Grades 7-10 and 81.8 percent among those with Grades 11-12. Women with no education were at 54.2 percent, although that estimate is based on a small number of cases and should be read cautiously. Even with that caution, the direction is clear: women with less schooling are much less likely to receive a postnatal check soon after birth.

sections.heading: Wealth matters, but the pattern is flatter than a simple top-to-bottom ladder
sections.body: Women in the lowest wealth quintile were at 71.6 percent in 2025, below the second quintile at 79.0 percent, the middle quintile at 79.4 percent, and the fourth quintile at 78.5 percent. But the highest quintile was not the highest on this measure; it was at 75.0 percent. That suggests household resources matter, but they do not explain the whole pattern on their own. The regional differences remain wider and more revealing than a simple richest-versus-poorest comparison.

sections.heading: Age gaps are present but modest
sections.body: Mothers younger than 20 were at 73.0 percent in 2025, compared with 77.0 percent among those aged 20-34 and 76.7 percent among those aged 35-49. The youngest group improved from 67.5 percent in 2022, but the overall spread by age remained narrow at about 4 percentage points. By comparison, the regional and education gaps are much wider, so age does not appear to be the main dividing line in this part of the data.

takeaways: Postnatal checks in the first 2 days after birth remain highly uneven across regions.
Education gaps are more pronounced than age gaps in early postnatal contact.
The national average changed little from 2022 to 2025, but several regions moved sharply in opposite directions.
